content.full_text:
stat. of C:alifornia 
Memorandum 
File ~o. ~-83-l47 
April 25, 1983 
TO: 
Ted Pr im 
FROM: 
Judy Davis 
RE: 
The Commission as Code-Reviewing Body for Article 2 
Filer s 
Article 2 of Chapter 7 (Conflicts of Interest) of the Act 
provides that certain named public officials, including county 
supervisors, city councilmembers, and elected state officers, 
make disclosure of specified financial interests. 
Section 87303 
requires that disclosure requirements for all designated 
employees, except Article 2 filers, must be included in a 
conflict of interest code. 
In reviewing the Conflict of 
Interest Code for the Controller's Office several years ago, the 
Commission aetermined that additional disclosure (beyond that 
for which Article 2 provides) may not be required of any 
Article 2 filer in any code adopted by the filer's agency. 
Both 
because of the provisions of Section 87303 and the Commission's 
decision, and to avoid burdening Article 2 filers with dupli-
cative filings, the model code (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730, 
adopted April 1980) exempts Article 2 filers from having to 
disclose pursuant to their agency code. 
In 1980, when the model code regulation was adopted, the Act 
provided that the sole remedy for a violation of 87100 (the 
basic conflict of interest provision) was injunctive. 
Administrative and civil actions in which remedies other than 
the injunctive relief set out in 91006 were sought had to be 
based not on a violation of 87100, but on the official's 
violation of the disqualification provisions of his agency 
code. 
Consequently, the model code provided that Article 2 
filers should be designated in an agency code, and covered "for 
disqualification purposes only," although not covered for 
disclosure purposes, if they were designated in their Article 2 
capacity, or if the jurisdlction of the agency was the same as 
or wholly included within the filer's jurisdiction as an 
Article 2 filer. 


Ted Prim 
Page 2 
From the inception of the FPPC through 1980, the Commission 
functioned as code-reviewing body for all codes for local 
Article 2 filers, including local codes for city councils or 
county boards of supervisors. 
The only designated employees in 
those local codes, as approved by the Commission, were the 
Article 2 filers. 
The other city or local officials, and 
Article 2 filers serving some other local capacity, were covered 
by local codes approved by the local code reviewing body. 
Effective January 1, 1981, Section 87102 of the Act was 
amended to make the remedies in Chapter 3 (administrative) and 
Chapter 9 (civil and criminal) available in enforcing 87100 
violations committed by any puolic official except an elected 
state officer. 
Enforcement actions charging Article 2 filers 
with conflict of interest violations could now be based on a 
violation of 87100; the enactment and review of a local code was 
no longer a condition precedent to an enforcement action in 
which other than injunctive relief was sought. 
Because of this change in the law, it was determined that it 
was no longer necessary for the Commission to review Codes 
covering Article 2 filers in their Article 2 capacityl/ or for 
Article 2 filers to be designated in their agency codes. 
The 
recent amendment to 91005 (effective 1/1/83) cleared up the last 
remaining ambiguity by specifying that damages could be 
recovered from public officials specified in 87200, as well as 
from "designated employees," in a civil action based on a 
conflict of interest violation. 
In its recent advice letter to the Coastal Commission, the 
Commission took the position that Secton 81013 is basically a 
pre-emption provision; although that section permits local 
legislative bodies such as city councils to impose additional 
requirements on Article 2 filers which go beyond what the Act 
specifies,~/ those additional requirements must be imposed by 
l/ The Commission may still review codes covering 
Article 2 persons in different capacities. 
E.g., county 
supervisor as member of joint powers agency with jurisdiction in 
more than one county. 
Local code reviewing bodies still review 
COdes including Article 2 filers serving in some other local 
capacity. 
~/ Such additional requirements are permissable only to 
the extent that they do not conflict with the provisions of the 
Act. 


Ted Prim 
Page 3 
local ordinance and cannot be enactea through the code-adoption 
process. 
This advice is consistent with the position taken by 
the Commission in the Alperin Opinion (1977) 3 FPPC 77, 79: 
"Section 81013 assures that the legislative authority of local 
jurisdictions is not unduly restricted by the Political Reform 
Act, but it does not endow local jurisdictions with the power to 
convert local violations into state violations through the 
vehicle of a conflict of interest code." 
In summary, the Commission's position now is that it need no 
longer function as code-reviewing body for codes covering 
Article 2 filers: 
as the Act itself provides both for dis-
closure and for remedies for conflict of interest violations, an 
agency code for Article 2 filers is essentially superfluousj 
since cities and counties have inherent legislative powers, they 
may impose additional requirements on their Article 2 filers 
without our review, so long as they do so tnrough some vehicle 
other than their code, and so long as the additional require-
ments do not conflict with the provisions of the Act. 
However, numerous sections of the Act have not been amended 
to clarify the Commission's position. 
For example, 82011(a) 
still provides that the Commission is the code-reviewing body 
for all codes covering Article 2 filersj 87300 still mandates 
that all agencies (defined in 82003 as including all local 
government agencies) adopt codes; 87303 still provides that no 
such codes shall be effective until reviewed by us. 
As a 
result, some city attorneys and county counsels appear to 
believe that agencies SUCh as city councils and boards of 
supervisors must still adopt codes covering Article 2 filers and 
that we must still approve them. 
In addition, the fact tnat the 
model code covers some officials who are Article 2 filers for 
disqualification purposes only may now be misleading. 
Section 
18730 provides both for Article 2 filers who are designated in a 
code in their Article 2 capacity (which is now unnecessary), and 
for Article 2 filers who are included in a code because they are 
acting in some other, non-Article 2 capacity (in which case they 
should still be included in the code, although, as 18730 points 
out, they often will not be given any additonal disclosure 
obligations under the code) . 
In some cases, an agency may propose different disclosure or 
disqualification provisions for its designated employees than 
those provided ln the Act. 
An agency may attempt to do this for 
either Article 2 filers or for non-Article 2 filers. 
In either 
case, the additional requirements must be reviewed by the 
appropriate code revlewing body (the FPPC in case of Article 2 
filers in their Art icle 2 capaci ty), which can approve the 


Ted Prim 
Page 4 
requirements only if they are consistent with and further the 
purposes of the Act. 
However, the Commission is very unlikely 
to approve additional disclosure or disqualification for filers 
in their Article 2 capacity. 
The Commission may advise local 
code reviewing bodies as to whether or not they should approve 
additional requirements for designated employees in local codes. 
Since some city and county clerks and attorneys persist in 
sending us copies of codes covering their Article 2 filers (some 
of which go beyond what the Act requires), and since local 
enforcers may also be confused about the status of those codes, 
it was agreed that better communication of the Commission's 
position and its rationale for that position to city and county 
attorneys and clerks and to district attorneys should be 
attempted. 
It was also agreed that "clean up" amendments to the 
Act and our regulations should be considered to avoid the 
confusion caused by the apparent statutory duties remaining "on 
the books." 
JD:km

sections.question_synthetic: This is an internal FPPC memorandum explaining the Commission's evolving position on whether it must serve as the code-reviewing body for conflict of interest codes covering Article 2 filers (elected officials like county supervisors and city councilmembers).
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC's position is that it no longer needs to function as code-reviewing body for codes covering Article 2 filers since the Act itself provides disclosure requirements and enforcement remedies; agencies may impose additional requirements through ordinance rather than codes, provided they do not conflict with the Act; however, statutory sections remain unchanged creating confusion among local officials.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memorandum rather than formal advice letter; no traditional Q/A format; document explains institutional policy positions and legal analysis; some OCR errors present ('C:alifornia', 'tnrough'); confidence reduced due to lack of standard advice letter structure but content clearly identifies FPPC legal positions

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: This is an internal FPPC memorandum explaining the Commission's evolving position on whether it must serve as the code-reviewing body for conflict of interest codes covering Article 2 filers (elected officials like county supervisors and city councilmembers).

CONCLUSION: The FPPC's position is that it no longer needs to function as code-reviewing body for codes covering Article 2 filers since the Act itself provides disclosure requirements and enforcement remedies; agencies may impose additional requirements through ordinance rather than codes, provided they do not conflict with the Act; however, statutory sections remain unchanged creating confusion among local officials.

embedding.summary: Internal FPPC memorandum from Judy Davis to Ted Prim analyzing the Commission's changing role regarding code review for Article 2 filers, explaining legal amendments that eliminated the necessity for FPPC code review while noting statutory provisions remain unchanged, causing ongoing confusion among local jurisdictions.
